cot_prefix: Okay, let me try to work through this question. The passage is about the copyright of the "Happy Birthday to You" song. The main issue here is whether the song is owned by Warner Music Group or if it's in the public domain. The question is asking what the history experts' statement proves about the "Happy Birthday" song. The options are A to D. Let me look back at the passage to find the relevant part. The passage says: "A number of history experts say that there is no record of who actually wrote the 'Happy Birthday to You' lyrics. Historians also say there is no way to know when the general public began singing the 'Happy Birthday' song, but they believe it was being sung by the public long before it was printed and owned by a company." So the experts are saying that there's no record of the original authors of the lyrics. Also, they believe the song was already being sung publicly before a company (presumably Warner Music Group) claimed ownership. The key point here is that if the song was being sung by the public long before it was printed and owned by a company, then it might not belong to Warner Music Group. That would support the idea that the song is in the public domain. The filmmakers' lawyers are arguing that the song belongs to everyone, not to Warner Music Group. Looking at the options: A) does not belong to Warner Music Group B) has more than 200 years' history C) was initially owned by another company D) has always been very popular The passage mentions that the original song "Good Morning to All" was written in 1893. The "Happy Birthday" lyrics were added later, but the exact origin is unclear. But 1893 is over 100 years ago, not 200, so B is probably incorrect. The passage doesn't mention another company initially owning the song, so C is not supported. D says "has always been very popular" but the passage doesn't discuss the song's popularity over time, just its ownership and history.
